keyEdge: Java language round, Kadane's, OOP, Banking domain critical
negotiationTip: Bonus is discretionary — get floor/ceiling in writing. Ask for ₹3–5L joining bonus to cover lost unvested Barclays benefits.

rounds.label: Online Assessment
rounds.overview: 90-minute HackerRank: 20 MCQs (Java output, OOP, DS complexity) + 2 coding problems. Kadane's confirmed in 5+ MS OA reports.
tips: String pool vs heap (== vs equals) is the most common MCQ trap
Kadane's Algorithm confirmed in 5+ rounds — know every variant
Static method hiding vs overriding is a frequent output-prediction trap
Hard problem follows Jump Game II greedy interval pattern

questions.answer:
Prints: true, false, true.

• a == b → true  — same interned literal in String pool
• a == c → false — new String() creates a heap object outside the pool
• a.equals(c) → true — equals() compares character values, not references

Key rule: Use == only for reference equality. Always use equals() for value comparison.
followUps: Where is the String pool in JVM memory?
questions.notes: Most common MS OA MCQ trap.

questions.answer:
Prints: 'Parent'.

Static methods are NOT overridden — they are hidden. Resolution is at compile-time based on the declared reference type (Parent), not runtime type (Child). Only instance methods support runtime dynamic dispatch.
questions.javaCode:
Parent p = new Child();
p.show(); // "Parent" — static, compile-time resolution
followUps: Can you use @Override on a static method?

questions.question:
Find the contiguous subarray with maximum sum.
Example: [-2,1,-3,4,-1,2,1,-5,4] → 6

Follow-up: Return actual subarray indices.
questions.answer:
Kadane's Algorithm: track maxEndingHere and maxSoFar. At each index, extend current or start fresh.
Time: O(n), Space: O(1).
followUps: Return actual subarray not just sum
What if all elements are negative?
questions.notes: Confirmed in MS OA across 5+ reports.

questions.question: Garden of size n, n+1 taps. ranges[i] = tap i waters [i-ranges[i], i+ranges[i]]. Minimum taps to water entire [0,n] or -1.
questions.answer:
Greedy interval covering — same pattern as Jump Game II.
1. Build maxReach[] array
2. Greedy scan with curEnd and curFarthest
3. Gap detected → return -1
Time: O(n), Space: O(n)
followUps: How is this similar to Jump Game II?

rounds.label: Java Deep Dive
rounds.overview: MS's most distinctive round — unique among all 15 companies. Pure Java internals for 60 min. Interviewer probes deeper on everything you mention.
tips: Sort employees by ID confirmed in 5+ MS India interviews
Sort HashMap by values caught a candidate off guard
Close ConcurrentHashMap answer with Barclays FX cache example
Expression evaluator is coded live — write clean commented code

questions.topic: Sort Employees by ID — Java 8 Streams
questions.question: Given List<Employee> (id, name, department, salary), sort by ID using Java 8 streams. Extend: department asc then salary desc. Extend: null-safe on name.
questions.answer:
Comparator.comparingInt() for single field. Chain .thenComparing() for multi-field. nullsFirst/nullsLast for null safety.

Key: stream.sorted() returns new stream, does NOT mutate. list.sort() mutates in place. TimSort is stable.
followUps: Same ID — stable sort preserves original order
questions.notes: Single most confirmed MS Java round question — 5+ reports.

questions.question: Given HashMap<String, Integer>, sort by values ascending. Return LinkedHashMap preserving sorted order.
questions.answer:
Extract entrySet → stream sort by value → collect into LinkedHashMap.
Most common mistake: collecting into HashMap — order lost immediately.
questions.javaCode:
Map<String, Integer> sorted = scores.entrySet().stream()
    .sorted(Map.Entry.comparingByValue())
    .collect(Collectors.toMap(
        Map.Entry::getKey, Map.Entry::getValue,
        (e1, e2) -> e1,
        LinkedHashMap::new  // MUST be LinkedHashMap
    ));
followUps: TreeMap sorts by key or value?
What is the merge function for?
questions.notes: Caught a MS candidate completely off guard.

questions.topic: HashSet vs TreeSet vs LinkedHashSet Internals
questions.question: Explain internal implementation of HashSet, TreeSet, LinkedHashSet. Time complexities and ordering guarantees?
questions.answer:
HashSet: backed by HashMap (elements=keys, value=PRESENT). No order. O(1) avg. One null allowed.
TreeSet: backed by TreeMap (Red-Black Tree). Sorted. O(log n). No null.
LinkedHashSet: backed by LinkedHashMap. Insertion-ordered. O(1). Best of both.
questions.javaCode:
Set<String> hash   = new HashSet<>();    // unordered,        O(1)
Set<String> tree   = new TreeSet<>();    // sorted,           O(log n)
Set<String> linked = new LinkedHashSet<>(); // insertion-order, O(1)

questions.question: Difference between ConcurrentHashMap and Collections.synchronizedMap()? When to use each?
questions.answer:
synchronizedMap: single lock for entire map. All reads/writes block each other.
ConcurrentHashMap: CAS + bucket-level locking. Lock-free reads. No null keys/values. Atomic ops: putIfAbsent(), computeIfAbsent(), merge().

Always prefer ConcurrentHashMap for high-throughput financial services.
followUps: Why no null keys in ConcurrentHashMap?
questions.notes: Always close with Barclays FX cache example.

questions.question: Evaluate expression string where operator priorities are given externally via Map<Character, Integer>. Left-to-right associativity. Code it live.
questions.answer:
Modified Shunting-Yard with two stacks: operands and operators.
Key design: external priority map demonstrates OCP — new operators add without changing evaluator.
questions.notes: Coded live — write clean commented code. External priority map is the design insight MS looks for.

rounds.label: DSA / Coding
rounds.overview: ONE problem, very deep. MS wants brute force → optimal progression with complexities at every step. Verbal reasoning weighted equally to code quality.
tips: Always start with brute force — MS explicitly wants the progression
State time/space at EVERY approach
'This is Fibonacci' meta-observation impresses
DP: recursion → memo → bottom-up → space-optimised
Linked list: ask if you can copy node values

questions.question: Count ways to climb n stairs taking 1 or 2 steps. Walk through ALL approaches with time/space at each step.
questions.answer:
Meta-observation: this IS the Fibonacci sequence — state this.
1. Brute force recursion: O(2^n), O(n)
2. Memoisation: O(n), O(n)
3. Bottom-up DP: O(n), O(n)
4. Space-optimised: O(n), O(1)
5. Matrix exponentiation: O(log n), O(1) — for n up to 10^18
questions.notes: Confirmed in MS DSA round. 'This is Fibonacci' consistently impresses.

questions.topic: Delete Linked List Node in O(1)
questions.question: Given only a reference to a node (not head), delete it in O(1) time and O(1) space.
questions.answer:
Copy next node's value into current, skip next.
Constraint: only works if node is NOT the tail.
followUps: What if node is the tail?

questions.question: Find indices of two numbers summing to target. Must be O(n).
questions.answer: HashMap: store value→index. For each element check if complement exists.

questions.question: Detect cycle in directed graph. Implement topological sort.
questions.answer:
Three-colour DFS: WHITE=unvisited, GRAY=in-stack, BLACK=done. GRAY→GRAY edge = cycle.
Kahn's topological sort: BFS with in-degree array.

rounds.label: System Design
rounds.overview: HLD + LLD mix. Key MS trait: requirements change mid-design. Never restart — acknowledge, state impact, extend. Finance systems are usual context.
tips: Changing requirements: acknowledge → impact → extend. Never restart
Relate every design to Barclays experience
Lock ordering by account ID mandatory for banking LLD
REST for client-facing, gRPC for internal services

questions.topic: Online Banking System — LLD with Concurrency
questions.question: Design online banking system with LLD and OOP. Handle race conditions on balance and deadlocks on transfers. Requirements will change mid-design.
questions.answer:
Account: ReentrantLock + volatile balance. Transaction: immutable. TransferService: lock ordering.
Deadlock prevention: always acquire locks in account-ID alphabetical order (breaks circular wait).
Extensions: multi-currency (Money value object), interest (ScheduledExecutorService), overdraft (subclass).

questions.question: Normalised DB for payroll. Salary = Basic+HRA+PA. Multiple bank accounts with % splits summing to 100%.
questions.answer:
Company → Department → Employee → SalaryComponent (1:1) + BankAccount (1:N).
Constraint: SUM(percentage) per employee = 100.
questions.pseudoCode:
Tables:
• Employee(id, name, dept_id FK, designation, joining_date)
• SalaryComponent(emp_id PK FK, basic, hra, pa, effective_from)
• BankAccount(id, emp_id FK, account_number UNIQUE, percentage DECIMAL(5,2))

Disbursement query:
SELECT e.name, ba.account_number,
       (sc.basic+sc.hra+sc.pa)*ba.percentage/100 AS amount
FROM Employee e
JOIN SalaryComponent sc ON e.id=sc.emp_id
JOIN BankAccount ba ON e.id=ba.emp_id;

rounds.label: Hiring Manager
rounds.overview: 45-min techno-managerial mix. MS checks: self-awareness, responsible AI tool use, and whether they'd trust you to drive a project independently.
tips: MS checks LeetCode profile — have a reasonable count
Lead every answer with Barclays experience
Regulated SDLC detail (CAB, compliance gates) strongly differentiates
Performance answers need real numbers: before/after

questions.topic: Complex Project + What Would You Redesign
questions.question: Tell me about a complex project. If redesigning today, what would you change?
questions.answer:
2-min project description → 2-3 specific improvements.

Barclays settlement reconciliation:
(1) Event sourcing — immutable audit trail for FCA compliance
(2) Decouple reconciliation engine from reporting
(3) Distributed tracing from day one — debugging cost significant man-hours
followUps: Why not make those changes at the time?

questions.question: Describe a performance bottleneck you resolved. Include initial issue, analysis, fix, and measured results.
questions.answer:
Barclays reconciliation: 4 hours on 1M transactions, near 6-hour SLA window.
Java Flight Recorder → 70% time in nested loop matching — O(n²).
Fix: HashMap keyed by transaction ref → O(1) lookup, O(n) total.
Result: 4 hours → 18 min → 4.5 min with parallelStream().

questions.question: What are deadlocks? How to detect in production? How to prevent? Give a real example.
questions.answer:
Coffman conditions: mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait.

Prevention: lock ordering (by account ID), tryLock with timeout, lock-free structures.
Detection: jstack <pid> → thread dump → look for BLOCKED wait cycles.

Barclays: Thread A locks Acc1→Acc2, Thread B locks Acc2→Acc1. Fix: sort by ID before acquiring. Added as SonarQube custom rule.
